answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical demonstration. Gen 1:26-27 betsalmenu kidmutenu: tselem H6754 (image, representational likeness) paired with demuth H1823 (likeness, similitude). The pairing is repeated in Gen 5:1-3 (Adam made bidmut elohim, then fathered Seth bidmuto k'tsalmo), and Gen 9:6 grounds the prohibition on bloodshed in betselem elohim asah et ha-adam (in the image of God he made man). Jas 3:9 homoiōsis G3669 (likeness) repeats the lexical pattern in Greek: men are made kath homoiōsin theou. 1 Cor 11:7 names man as eikōn G1504 kai doxa theou. The Pauline corpus then ties human imago Dei to Christology and soteriology: Christ is the eikōn of the invisible God (Col 1:15; 2 Cor 4:4), believers are conformed to the eikōn of his Son (Rom 8:29), and the new self is being renewed kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton (Col 3:10; cf. Eph 4:24). The lexical chain tselem and demuth in Hebrew to eikōn and homoiōsis in Greek runs continuously from creation to new creation.
answer.notes: The imago Dei is foundational Christian anthropology and grounds three load-bearing structures: the sanctity of human life (Gen 9:6 explicitly), the dignity of every human being regardless of age, ability, ethnicity, or development (Jas 3:9 forbids cursing people on this basis), and the Christological logic of redemption (Christ is the eikōn of God, and salvation is the renewal of that image in the believer, Col 3:10, Rom 8:29). On the cult-marker question: denial of the imago Dei would be a serious heresy, but public-record carriers of explicit denial within Christianity are virtually nonexistent. Even groups historically classed as heterodox by mainstream Christianity (Mormonism, Jehovah's Witnesses, Christian Science, Christadelphians, Iglesia ni Cristo, Oneness Pentecostals, Unitarian Universalists) affirm the imago Dei in some form, though with widely divergent metaphysical interpretations. Mormonism in particular interprets the image hyper-literally (God has a physical body, man is God's literal offspring), but this is a distortion rather than a denial. The doctrine therefore clears would_die_for on its canonical strength, but cult_marker_if_denied is set false because the public-record landscape gives no settled denomination that denies it; the cult-marker tier is reserved for boundaries Scripture itself names (1 John 4:2-3 on incarnation) or that mark out an active heretical movement. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: the imago Dei is universally affirmed at the level of confession, but it is widely violated at the level of fruit. Any teaching or culture that demeans people made in the image of God by ethnicity, by ability, by age, by sex, by unborn status, by criminal status, or by class is functionally undermining the doctrine even while affirming it verbally. Jas 3:9 makes the practical test sharp: we bless God with the same tongue we use to curse people made in his likeness, and the apostle calls this incoherent. The Irenaean image-likeness distinction (image = ontological capacity retained after the Fall, likeness = moral conformity lost and restored in Christ) is one historical attempt to systematize the two Hebrew terms, but the lexical evidence does not require it; tselem and demuth function as parallel synonyms in Gen 1:26 and the order is reversed in Gen 5:1-3, suggesting the early biblical writers used them as a near-synonymous word pair rather than denoting two distinct realities. The doctrine is unanimously confessed across Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist, Anabaptist, and Pentecostal lineages.

stem_audit.notes: The statement asserts the verdict but the assertion is a plain gloss of the lexical anchors in Gen 1:26-27, Gen 9:6, and Jas 3:9. The expansion 'inherent dignity, moral agency, rationality, and capacity for relationship with God' is theological commentary on the lexical content; the bare lexical claim (humans are made betselem elohim, in the image of God) is what Scripture supplies. The qualifier 'irrespective of age, ability, ethnicity, or stage of development' is a pastoral universal that the lexical evidence supports through the unrestricted reference to ha-adam (humanity) in Gen 1:26 and the unrestricted reference to anthropoi (people, men) in Jas 3:9. No confessional smuggling.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: every human being is made in the image of God. This is not earned, it is not lost by age or disability or ethnicity, and it does not depend on a stage of development before birth or any quality after birth. Every person bears the imprint of the Creator. The strongest reason from Scripture is direct and pan-canonical. Genesis 1:26 has God say, 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness,' and Genesis 1:27 says God did exactly that, making them male and female. Genesis 5:1-3 repeats it. Genesis 9:6 grounds the prohibition on murder in this very fact: whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, because God made man in his own image. James 3:9 in the New Testament repeats the same logic for our speech: we bless God with the same tongue we use to curse people made in the likeness of God, and James says this is incoherent. First Corinthians 11:7 calls man the image and glory of God. Paul connects this back to Christ: Christ himself is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15, Second Corinthians 4:4), and salvation is described as being conformed to the image of God's Son (Romans 8:29) and as having the new self renewed after the image of its creator (Colossians 3:10, Ephesians 4:24). The strongest counterargument inside the Bible is that the Fall in Genesis 3 damaged human nature and Paul writes that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Some readers conclude from this that the image of God was destroyed at the Fall and only restored in those who believe in Christ. The Bible answers this question directly. After the Fall, after the Flood, after every example of human evil, Genesis 9:6 and James 3:9 still ground human dignity in the image of God present in every human being. The image is defaced, not erased. Even fallen humans are still image-bearers, which is why murder of any person, and cursing of any person, is so serious. The image is being renewed in Christ, but the image is also still present in everyone, which is why every human life has worth.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage confesses that humans are made in the image of God. This is one of the most universally agreed doctrines in Christian anthropology. The early fathers wrote on it extensively. Irenaeus in the second century distinguished between image (which he located in the natural human capacities and which he taught was retained after the Fall) and likeness (which he located in the original supernatural communion with God and which he taught was lost at the Fall and restored in Christ). Athanasius wrote in On the Incarnation that humans were given a share in God's own image, namely in Christ, and that the whole purpose of the incarnation was to restore that defaced image. Augustine in On the Trinity located the image in human rationality, memory, and will, finding a trinitarian trace in the human soul. Catholic teaching follows this trajectory; the Catechism in paragraphs 355 through 361 confesses that man is the only creature on earth that God has willed for its own sake, called to share in God's own life by knowledge and love. The Orthodox Church confesses that humans are uniquely created in the image and likeness of God and are called to become like Christ, the uncreated Image of God. The Lutheran tradition in the Augsburg Confession and the Formula of Concord confesses humans created in the image of God but stresses that the moral image was profoundly damaged at the Fall. The Anglican 39 Articles affirm creation in God's image while emphasizing in Article 9 that original sin has marred human nature. The Reformed confessions (Westminster, Belgic, Heidelberg) confess creation in the image of God with knowledge, righteousness, and holiness, distinguishing a structural image retained from the Fall and a functional image lost and being restored in Christ. The Methodist Articles of Religion and the Anabaptist Dordrecht and Schleitheim confessions affirm the same. The Pentecostal Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths affirms human creation in God's image and the Fall that disrupted but did not erase it. Public-record carriers of full explicit denial within Christianity are essentially nonexistent. Mormonism affirms the image but interprets it as literal physical resemblance to a corporeal God, which mainstream Christianity classifies as a serious distortion rather than a denial. Jehovah's Witnesses affirm the image at the level of confession but redefine the persons in ways that affect other doctrines. Christian Science under Mary Baker Eddy affirms a spiritual image while denying material reality, which functionally distorts the doctrine. None of these groups says outright that humans are not made in the image of God; they all affirm the bare doctrine and dispute what it means. Outside Christianity, materialist atheism rejects the doctrine entirely, but no historic Christian movement does.

scripture.force: tselem H6754 = image, representational likeness, the same term used elsewhere for physical idols or statues that represent a being. demuth H1823 = likeness, similitude, resemblance. The two nouns are paired with prepositions be- (in) and ki- (according to). 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness.' Plural cohortative na'aseh (let us make) and plural suffixes -nu (our) supply the lexical foundation of the doctrine

scripture.key_term: וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ (vayivra elohim et ha-adam betsalmo) H6754
scripture.force: tselem H6754 repeated three times in one verse, once with first-person suffix (in his image) and twice in absolute construct (in the image of God). 'So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him, male and female he created them.' Triple lexical insistence with explicit application to both sexes

scripture.key_term: בִּדְמוּת אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֹתוֹ (bidmut elohim asah oto) H1823
scripture.force: demuth H1823 repeated from Gen 1:26 but now used alone without tselem. 'In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God he made him.' The pairing is not strict; the two terms function as a near-synonymous word pair, and either can stand for the whole concept

The same lexical pair as Gen 1:26 but applied to human procreation: Adam fathered Seth bidmuto k'tsalmo (in his own likeness, after his image). The order is reversed from Gen 1:26 (image then likeness) to (likeness then image), confirming that the two are a near-synonymous word pair rather than distinct theological categories. The image transmits through procreation

scripture.key_term: כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת־הָאָדָם (ki betselem elohim asah et ha-adam) H6754
scripture.force: tselem H6754 grounds the prohibition on murder. 'Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for in the image of God he made man.' The imago Dei is preserved after the Fall (this is a post-Flood pronouncement to Noah) and is the canonical foundation for the sanctity of human life. The conjunction ki (for, because) makes the image the explicit ground of the prohibition

scripture.key_term: τοὺς καθ' ὁμοίωσιν Θεοῦ γεγονότας (tous kath' homoiōsin theou gegonotas) G3669
scripture.force: homoiōsis G3669 = likeness, resemblance (the same Greek noun used in the Septuagint of Gen 1:26 for demuth). gegonotas = having become (perfect participle of ginomai G1096). 'With the tongue we bless God, and with the same tongue we curse people who have been made according to the likeness of God.' James's lexical pickup from Gen 1:26 LXX and his explicit application to the post-Fall, post-Christ era confirms the image is preserved in every human being

scripture.key_term: εἰκὼν καὶ δόξα Θεοῦ (eikōn kai doxa theou) G1504 + G1391
scripture.force: eikōn G1504 = image, representational likeness. doxa G1391 = glory. Paul names man as the image and glory of God. The phrase is embedded in an argument about head coverings that complicates a flat reading (Paul also says woman is the glory of man), but the lexical fact remains: anthrōpos as such bears the eikōn of God. The verse should be read with Gen 1:27 (male and female together bear the image)

scripture.key_term: εἰκὼν τοῦ Θεοῦ τοῦ ἀοράτου (eikōn tou theou tou aoratou) G1504
scripture.force: eikōn G1504 applied to Christ: he is 'the image of the invisible God.' This is the Christological pole of the imago Dei. Christ is the perfect, original, uncreated Image; humanity is the created derivative image. The doctrine of human imago Dei finds its meaning and its restoration in him

scripture.key_term: εἰκὼν τοῦ Θεοῦ (eikōn tou theou) G1504
scripture.force: eikōn G1504 applied to Christ: 'Christ, who is the image of God.' Parallel to Col 1:15. The gospel is named as 'the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God.' Confirms that the New Testament reads the human image as Christologically anchored

scripture.key_term: ἀνακαινούμενον... κατ' εἰκόνα τοῦ κτίσαντος αὐτόν (anakainoumenon kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton) G1504 + G341
scripture.force: anakainoō G341 = to renew, make new. Present passive participle, ongoing action. 'Being renewed in knowledge according to the image of him who created him.' Paul presents redemption as the renewal of the imago Dei, anchored back in Gen 1:26 by the explicit reference to 'him who created him.' The image is both retained (in every human) and being restored (in believers)

scripture.key_term: τὸν καινὸν ἄνθρωπον τὸν κατὰ Θεὸν κτισθέντα (ton kainon anthrōpon ton kata theon ktisthenta) G2537 + G2936
scripture.force: kainos G2537 = new (qualitatively, not just chronologically). ktizō G2936 = to create. 'The new self, created after God in true righteousness and holiness.' Paul echoes Gen 1:26 LXX (kat eikona theou) in the phrase kata theon. The new creation in Christ is a remaking of the human according to the divine pattern that was the original creation

scripture.key_term: συμμόρφους τῆς εἰκόνος τοῦ Υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (symmorphous tēs eikonos tou huiou autou) G4832 + G1504
scripture.force: symmorphos G4832 = having the same form, conformed. eikōn G1504 = image. 'Predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son.' The telos of salvation is conformity to the eikōn of Christ. Since Christ is himself the eikōn of God (Col 1:15), this is the restoration of the original imago Dei in humanity

'You have made him a little lower than the elohim (God, or heavenly beings), and crowned him with glory and honor.' The psalm celebrates human dignity and dominion in language that echoes Gen 1:26-28. The image and likeness language is not used explicitly, but the conceptual content (humanity exalted just below God, crowned with glory, given dominion) is the functional expression of the imago Dei in poetic register

'Behold, the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil.' After the Fall, God still speaks of humanity as like the divine council. The image is not erased by the Fall; it is qualified by the knowledge of good and evil. This complicates any reading that says the image was lost entirely in Genesis 3

scripture.key_term: ὑστεροῦνται τῆς δόξης τοῦ Θεοῦ (hysterountai tēs doxēs tou theou) G5302 + G1391
scripture.force: hystereō G5302 = to fall short, to lack. doxa G1391 = glory. 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.' Some readers take this to mean the imago Dei was destroyed at the Fall. But the verb hystereō is present middle, ongoing falling short, not past complete destruction. Paired with Jas 3:9 (which still names the post-Fall person as made in the likeness of God) and Gen 9:6 (post-Fall, post-Flood, image still grounds sanctity of life), the imago Dei is best read as defaced and being restored, not destroyed

scripture.key_term: τὴν εἰκόνα τοῦ χοϊκοῦ... τὴν εἰκόνα τοῦ ἐπουρανίου (tēn eikona tou choikou... tou epouraniou) G1504 + G5517 + G2032
scripture.force: eikōn G1504 repeated. 'Just as we have borne the image of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven.' Paul reads the imago Dei eschatologically: the earthy image (Adam in his fallen Adamic state) gives way to the heavenly image (the resurrection body conformed to Christ). The image is dynamic, with a current condition and a future fullness

scripture.key_term: τὴν αὐτὴν εἰκόνα μεταμορφούμεθα (tēn autēn eikona metamorphoumetha) G1504 + G3339
scripture.force: metamorphoō G3339 = to transform, transfigure. Present passive. 'We are being transformed into the same image from glory to glory.' The image (eikōn) is the same eikōn of the Lord into which believers are being transformed. The progressive sanctification is the restoration of the imago Dei

hermeneutics.notes: Verdict rests on a pan-canonical lexical network from creation (Gen 1:26-27, Gen 5:1-3, Gen 9:6) through wisdom (Ps 8) through prophets (the imago Dei underlies the prohibitions on idolatry and on murder throughout the Torah and prophets) through epistles (Jas 3:9, 1 Cor 11:7, 1 Cor 15:49, 2 Cor 3:18, 2 Cor 4:4, Rom 8:29, Col 1:15, Col 3:10, Eph 4:24). Every framework in play (covenant theology, dispensationalism, new covenant theology, progressive covenantalism, redemptive-historical) confesses the doctrine identically; no competing-lens verdicts to record. Analogia scripturae is invoked at the resolution of the Fall complication: Gen 3:22 and Jas 3:9 together establish that the image survives the Fall, while Rom 3:23 and Col 3:10 together establish that the image is defaced and being restored. Progressive revelation operates in the Christological intensification: the Old Testament establishes humans as image-bearers; the New Testament reveals Christ as the original Image after which humans were patterned and into which they are being conformed. The relevant lemmas are tselem H6754 (17 occurrences), demuth H1823 (25 occurrences), eikōn G1504 (23 NT occurrences), homoiōsis G3669 (NT hapax in Jas 3:9 but the LXX equivalent for demuth in Gen 1:26). The Irenaean image-likeness distinction is one historical attempt to systematize the two Hebrew terms, but the lexical evidence (Gen 1:26 pairs them, Gen 5:1 uses demuth alone, Gen 9:6 uses tselem alone, Gen 5:3 reverses the order) shows they are a near-synonymous word pair, not two distinct realities. The distinction is acceptable as theological synthesis but cannot be required from the text alone.

counter_witness.key_phrase: Man, and not part of man, was made in the likeness of God. The soul and the spirit are part of the man, but they certainly are not the man, for the perfect man consists in the commingling and union of the soul receiving the Spirit of the Father
The good God has given them a share in His own Image, that is, in our Lord Jesus Christ, and has made even themselves after the same Image and Likeness. The incarnation restores the defaced image

counter_witness.anchor: Augustine, On the Trinity XIV.4 (c. 419 AD)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The image of God is in that part of man's mind by which it cleaves to the eternal Reason that it may consult it. Located the image in human rationality, memory, and will, the so-called psychological analogy
Man occupies a unique place in creation. He is in the image of God. In his own nature he unites the spiritual and material worlds. He is created in the image of God; he is capable of self-knowledge

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith, Doctrine, Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: The human person is the only one created in the image and likeness of God. Humans are called to become like Christ, the uncreated Image of God, and to share in all the spiritual attributes of divinity

counter_witness.anchor: Formula of Concord, Solid Declaration I, On Original Sin
counter_witness.key_phrase: Man was originally created by God pure and holy, in the image of God, but lost original righteousness through the Fall, while remaining human and bearing the structural image that grounds his accountability to God

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article IX, Of Original or Birth-Sin
counter_witness.key_phrase: Original sin is the fault and corruption of the Nature of every man, whereby man is very far gone from original righteousness, and is of his own nature inclined to evil. Presupposes original righteousness in the image of God
After God had made all other creatures, he created man, male and female, with reasonable and immortal souls, endued with knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness, after his own image
God created man good and after His own image, that is, in true righteousness and holiness, that he might rightly know God his Creator, heartily love Him, and live with Him in eternal blessedness

We believe that God created man from the dust of the earth and made and formed him in his own image and likeness, good, just, and holy, so that he could by his will conform to the will of God in every way

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion, Article VII, Of Original or Birth Sin
counter_witness.key_phrase: Original sin is the corruption of the nature of every man, whereby man is very far gone from original righteousness, and of his own nature inclined to evil. Presupposes original righteousness in the imago Dei

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession Article 1, Of God and the Creation of Man (1632)
counter_witness.key_phrase: God created the first man, the father of us all, Adam, of the dust of the earth, in his own image and likeness, holy, good, righteous, immortal, free, and possessed of all that could be desired

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, The Fall of Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Man was created good and upright, for God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness. However, man by voluntary transgression fell, and his only hope of redemption is in Christ

web.quote: Gen 1:26 betsalmenu kidmutenu: tselem H6754 (image) paired with demuth H1823 (likeness). 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness.' Plural cohortative na'aseh with plural suffixes
Gen 1:27 tselem H6754 three times in one verse: in his image, in the image of God, male and female. Triple lexical insistence with explicit application to both sexes
Gen 5:3 bidmuto k'tsalmo: Adam fathered Seth in his own likeness, after his image. Order reversed from Gen 1:26, confirming the two terms function as a near-synonymous word pair
Gen 9:6 ki betselem elohim asah et ha-adam: 'for in the image of God he made man.' The post-Flood prohibition on murder is grounded explicitly in the surviving imago Dei
Jas 3:9 tous kath' homoiōsin theou gegonotas: people who have been made according to the likeness of God. homoiōsis G3669 is the Greek noun used in LXX Gen 1:26 for demuth
Col 1:15 eikōn tou theou tou aoratou: Christ is the image of the invisible God. The Christological pole of the imago Dei doctrine
Col 3:10 anakainoumenon kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton: being renewed according to the image of him who created him. Explicit reference back to Gen 1:26, salvation as renewal of the imago Dei
2 Cor 4:4 eikōn tou theou: Christ is the image of God. Parallel to Col 1:15, confirms the Christological anchor of the imago Dei

tselem H6754, 17 occurrences. Used for the divine image in humanity (Gen 1:26-27, 5:3, 9:6) and for idolatrous images elsewhere, with the negative use grounded in the unique legitimacy of God's own image-making

demuth H1823, 25 occurrences. Used for the divine likeness in humanity (Gen 1:26, 5:1, 5:3) and for visionary likenesses (Ezekiel 1 and 10). Paired with tselem in Gen 1:26 and Gen 5:3 as near-synonyms
eikōn G1504, 23 NT occurrences. Applied to Christ (Col 1:15, 2 Cor 4:4), to humanity (1 Cor 11:7, 1 Cor 15:49), to the new self renewed in salvation (Col 3:10, Rom 8:29, 2 Cor 3:18)

Athanasius On the Incarnation 3: the good God has given them a share in His own Image, namely Christ, and has made even themselves after the same Image and Likeness. Incarnation restores defaced image
Orthodox Church in America: humans are the only one created in the image and likeness of God, called to become like Christ, the uncreated Image of God, sharing in the spiritual attributes of divinity
39 Articles IX: man is very far gone from original righteousness, and is of his own nature inclined to evil. Presupposes original creation in the image of God which the Fall has marred
